title: podman dns does not work for containers that have been created but are not started.
body:
### Issue Description

I am using docker-compose with podman.
I have a bridge network for container communication.

Each container has an alias in the network specified.

When I `up` the env the containers get created.
However, if one of them doesn't start or exits unexpectedly, the dns server will not resolve that host.

This leads to issues with my nginx reverse proxy where it won't start if one of the containers in the group is down.

It would be nice if the ip host mapping was cached as long as the container was still around (even if off)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. create a compose.yml file with 2 containers both joined to the network with aliases
2. docker-compose up --no-start
3. start only one of them
4. try to resolve the other hostname


### Describe the results you received

The other hosts cannot be resolved when they are down.

### Describe the results you expected

it would be nice for it to continue to resolve to the last known address...  or even better if containers were assigned ip addresses on container creation instead.

### podman info output

### Additional information

comments.body:
If anything this is a feature request as this works as designed.

I don't understand the use case here at all. What you are asking sounds wrong. Assigning addresses must happen on start, you can have more containers than available ips on a network so this would just break if we start to assign ips on creation.

Second resolving any address for something which is not currently running is a bad idea. We would just resolve names for something which is not reachable. That may make your nginx config happy but I really do not see how this would not lead to confusion for other callers: `name xxx not found` is a much better error message then `connection timed out`.

This really sound like you should just fix you nginx config, e.g. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32845674/setup-nginx-not-to-crash-if-host-in-upstream-is-not-found

So overall I don't think this is a good idea and you you should fix it on your application level.

@Luap99 

> What you are asking sounds wrong. Assigning addresses must happen on start, you can have more containers than available ips on a network so this would just break if we start to assign ips on creation.

Except that this is how networks work in the "real world" all the time. 
DHCP servers dole out leases with expirations that outlive the uptime of any of the connected machines unless not renewed.

DNS entries have TTL settings which sets an expected cache time for if the ip changes quickly or not.

> Second resolving any address for something which is not currently running is a bad idea. 

This happens in the real world every day, all the time.  DNS is _completley_ decoupled from the uptime of an endpoint.

> This really sound like you should just fix you nginx config

I agree that this behavior in nginx is stupid, and I will look into fixing it.   But I maintain that this behavior in podman is actually the undesired (at least as the _default_) behavior.

If you want to try a simple test.   Plug a new computer into your router and let it get a DCHP lease.
Then unplug it and have another machine try to ping it via the hostname that used to work when it was up.

You'll get `connection timed out`.

I am very well aware how this works but for an internal dns server this makes no sense, it shouldn't return addresses which do not exists. IP addresses are assigned when you start the container and removed when it is stopped/exits. There is no way to implement what you are asking with the current design how networking works in podman.

Unless you specify a static address there is no point in even continuing to serve the old name when the container is stopped/exited, a new start will get very likely a new ip address thus the "cached" name would point at the wrong ip.
